blocks.hero_h1: Import a 2024 Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line
blocks.hero_subtitle: Bring in a 2024 Mercedes-Benz EQE 350 AMG Line EV from Japan with a 90.6-kWh battery and long-range comfort, set up for NZ roads and everyday driving.
proof_points: Transparent pricing — compliance is part of the process
blocks.why_kiwis_buy: The 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line pairs a 90.6-kWh battery with rear-drive efficiency, giving strong real-world range for NZ commutes and weekend trips. Regenerative braking helps in stop-start city traffic and on hilly routes, while the Burmester audio and heated power seats keep longer drives easy enough to live with.

savings_block.headline: What you could save vs NZ dealer pricing
savings_block.body: Recent 2024 Mercedes-Benz EQE 350 AMG Line examples out of Japan typically land in NZ from about $120,000 to $140,000 once you factor in auction price, shipping, NZ entry work and our fee. Comparable cars advertised locally are often higher, especially for low-kilometre, high-spec AMG Line models. We map out the full landed estimate up front, including shipping and compliance costs, so you can compare against NZ retail with clear numbers.

cta_form.headline: Find your EQE 350 AMG Line — we'll do the searching
cta_form.subtext: Fill in your details to get started. We'll search the Japanese auctions and come back with real options and pricing by email.

blocks.video_caption: Here’s a look at the EQE 350 AMG Line cabin, with the large central screen, Burmester speakers and heated power seats that make longer NZ drives easy.
blocks.main_usp_deep: With its 90.6-kWh battery and rear-drive layout, the EQE 350 AMG Line is set up for long, quiet NZ motorway runs as well as urban commuting. The estimated 480 km range means most owners can cover a full week of city use on a couple of charges, especially with strong regenerative braking in traffic. Safety tech like blind-spot monitoring, lane-keeping assist and automatic high-beam headlamps helps on night drives and open-road trips. Inside, the 15-speaker Burmester system, wireless charging and powered heated front seats make it a calm, tech-forward place to spend time.

buyer_persona.headline: Is this car right for you?
buyer_persona.body: You’re likely a tech-forward professional who wants a premium EV that slots into a busy life without fuss. You care about real-world range, charging at home or work, and having modern safety and comfort features for family or client trips. If you’re moving out of a non-EV executive sedan and want lower running costs without losing comfort, this EQE 350 AMG Line is a solid match.

trust_company.headline: Why import with ImportYourCar.co.nz?
trust_company.body: We’re an NZ-based team that’s been importing from Japan since 2009, with hundreds of EVs and late-model vehicles sourced over that time. You get full auction sheets, translations and our commentary before you decide to bid. We talk you through landed pricing, shipping, and NZ entry work so you know where your money’s going. For EVs like the EQE 350 AMG Line, we pay close attention to battery information and usage patterns in Japan to help you make a confident call.

car_specs_benefits.benefit: Gives an estimated 480 km range, which covers most NZ commutes and weekend trips with room to spare.
Smooth, quiet power delivery that suits open-road cruising and daily city use without torque steer.
High-quality audio for long motorway runs or traffic, making the cabin a comfortable place to work or relax.
Keeps you comfortable on winter mornings and longer drives, and makes it easy to find a good driving position.
Adds an extra layer of safety when changing lanes on busy NZ motorways and driving at night.

models_comparison.intro: Here’s how different EQE 350 AMG Line examples from Japan can vary and what that means in NZ.

rows.notes: The EQE 350 AMG Line is a new model, so you’re mainly looking at 2024-onwards cars. Being new, these typically meet the latest NZ entry standards, including ESC and safety requirements, if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted.

The EQE 350 AMG Line uses a 90.6-kWh battery with a rear-drive motor. Differences between cars are more about software, wheel size and equipment than the core battery, but usage patterns in Japan can still affect real-world range.

AMG Line cars may vary by wheel design, interior trim, audio options and driver-assistance packages. Some will have extra comfort features or upgraded infotainment, so checking the auction sheet and photos closely matters.

As a late-model EV, the EQE 350 AMG Line typically meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean. You’ll need to factor in any applicable CO2 charges and standard entry certification checks, but there’s no exhaust emissions testing like a non-EV car.

Being a premium EV, the EQE 350 AMG Line appears less often than mass-market models. That can mean waiting a little longer for the right colour, mileage and spec, but it also helps keep quality high when we shortlist cars.

steps.title: Tell us what you're after
steps.body: Fill in the form or message us. We confirm your brief and give you a cost breakdown before anything moves forward.

steps.title: We search the auctions
steps.body: We monitor USS, TAA, HAA and others. For EVs like the EQE 350 AMG Line, we check battery health records and usage patterns on each shortlisted car wherever that data is available.

steps.title: You approve, we bid
steps.body: We send you the auction sheet and our recommendation. Once you're happy, you pay a deposit and we bid on your behalf.

steps.title: Shipping to NZ
steps.body: Typically 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule. For less common models, allow a little extra time once the auction clears. We keep you updated along the way.

steps.title: Compliance and delivery
steps.body: The car goes through NZ entry certification and compliance. Once it's through, we arrange delivery or you can collect.

risks_solutions.intro: Importing a premium EV is straightforward, but it helps to know what to expect.

items.risk: You’re unsure how much the battery has degraded and what that means for range in NZ.
items.solution: We focus on cars with clear service records and, where available, battery health data from Japan. You see the documentation and our notes before bidding, so you can decide if the indicated range suits your driving.

items.risk: Hidden costs pushing the final price well above your budget.
items.solution: We lay out a landed estimate that includes auction price, shipping, insurance, NZ entry work, our fee and likely on-road costs. You can then set a firm bidding limit that reflects your real budget.

items.risk: Uncertainty about auction grades and what they mean for condition.
items.solution: We translate and explain the auction sheet, including grade, comments and any noted repairs. Photos and our commentary help you understand how the car compares to typical NZ dealer stock.

blocks.how_to_use_iyc: Tell us the year, budget and features you want in an EQE 350 AMG Line, and we’ll shortlist suitable cars from the Japanese auctions. You review the options, documentation and pricing, then decide if you’d like us to bid. It’s a low-pressure way to see what’s out there before committing.

faq_block.q: What real-world range can I expect from a 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line in NZ?
faq_block.a: The 90.6-kWh battery has an estimated range of about 480 km, but real-world numbers depend on speed, temperature, hills and how you drive. In NZ conditions, many owners will see less than the estimate, yet it’s still plenty for daily commuting and most weekend trips with sensible charging habits.

faq_block.q: How much does it cost to import a 2024 Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line from Japan?
faq_block.a: Recent examples typically land between $120,000 and $140,000, depending on mileage, condition and auction price. On top of the hammer price you’ll have shipping, insurance, NZ entry certification, our service fee and on-road costs such as registration and any CO2 charges.

faq_block.q: Will a Japanese-import EQE 350 AMG Line meet NZ entry standards?
faq_block.a: A 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line is a modern EV that in most cases meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. Final approval always depends on physical inspection and checks at NZ entry certification.

faq_block.q: How long does it take from winning an auction to having the car on my driveway?
faq_block.a: From a successful bid, shipping to NZ usually takes 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule, then you need time for port processing, entry certification and registration. Overall, many buyers see their car in roughly 6–10 weeks, but rare models or shipping delays can stretch that out.

faq_block.q: How do auction grades work for a car like the EQE 350 AMG Line?
faq_block.a: Japanese auctions grade both the body and interior, and note any repairs or damage. We send you the full auction sheet, photos and our explanation of the grade so you can see how tidy the car is compared with typical NZ stock before deciding to bid.

blocks.company_positioning: ImportYourCar.co.nz is an NZ-based vehicle import service that’s been operating since 2009. We focus on sourcing late-model cars and EVs from Japan with clear documentation and realistic landed pricing. Our role is to bridge the gap between Japanese auctions and NZ buyers who want more choice than local yards offer.

landed_cost_breakdown.intro: Your landed cost is the total to get a 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line from a Japanese auction to ready for registration in NZ.

rows.notes: The hammer price you pay for the EQE 350 AMG Line in Japan, plus local auction fees.
Transport from the Japanese port to NZ and marine insurance to cover the car while it’s on the water.
Port charges, entry certification, inspections, and any work needed to meet NZ standards, plus registration and plates.

auction_checklist.check: Battery information and any EV-related warnings on the auction sheet
auction_checklist.why: Gives early clues about battery health and whether there are known issues that could affect range or costs.

auction_checklist.check: Odometer reading and service history stamps
auction_checklist.why: Lower kilometres and regular servicing usually mean less wear on the battery and other components.

auction_checklist.check: Interior and exterior grades and comments
auction_checklist.why: Helps you understand cosmetic condition and any repairs, so you’re not surprised when the car arrives.
Larger wheels can look sharp but may slightly reduce range and increase tyre replacement costs.

auction_checklist.check: Photos of the infotainment and driver-assistance controls
auction_checklist.why: Confirms you’re getting features like the Burmester audio, navigation and safety systems you expect.

not_for_you_block.headline: Who this may not suit
items: You need a seven-seat family vehicle or regular towing capacity beyond what a sedan EV is designed for.
You often drive very long distances in remote areas with limited fast-charging options and don’t want to plan charging stops.
You’re looking for the lowest possible purchase price and aren’t concerned about premium features or brand.

alternatives_block.intro: If the EQE 350 AMG Line doesn’t quite line up with your needs, there are other EVs worth considering.

rows.better_if: You want a larger liftback body and access to Tesla’s Supercharger network.
rows.tradeoff: Cabin feel and ride quality are different, and some buyers prefer the Mercedes interior and brand experience.

battery_health_block.headline: Battery health on the EQE 350 AMG Line
battery_health_block.body: On a 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line, the 90.6-kWh battery is new-generation tech, but usage in Japan still matters. We look at kilometres, service records and any available diagnostics to build a picture of how the battery has been treated. That helps you judge whether the likely range and long-term running costs line up with how you’ll use the car in NZ.
checklist: Review any available battery state-of-health or range reports from the Japanese seller or service records.
Check odometer and usage pattern — lots of short city trips vs longer motorway runs can affect battery wear differently.
Confirm there are no EV system warnings or error codes shown in dash photos.
Consider your own charging habits in NZ and how they’ll impact long-term battery health.

charging_block.headline: Charging a Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line in NZ
charging_block.home_charging: Most EQE 350 AMG Line owners in NZ will charge at home overnight using a wallbox. That turns the 90.6-kWh battery into a simple plug-in routine, and if you’re on a night-rate power plan, home charging costs are typically far lower than running a similar non-EV executive sedan.
charging_block.public_charging: For longer trips, you can top up at public AC and DC chargers around NZ. Planning stops on main routes makes it straightforward to cover intercity drives, and the car’s navigation can help you find charging locations on the way.
caveats: If you live in an apartment or don’t have off-street parking, you’ll need a clear plan for regular charging.
Public fast-charger availability can vary by region, so it’s worth checking coverage on your usual long routes.
Charging speeds and costs differ between networks, so factor that into your running-cost expectations.
